data.title: Energy soars as the strongest S&P 500 sector in Q2
data.description:
The Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLE), which tracks the S&P 500 energy sector, surged over 34% in the first quarter of 2026, heavily outperforming the Wall Street’s benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) index, which slipped nearly 5% during the quarter.

The fund, which includes companies involved in oil production, drilling, refining, and transportation, was the biggest quarterly gainer in the S&P 500 sectors, as oil prices soared in Q1 over geopolitical tensions.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) surged a whopping 80% in the last three months, compared to a 2.3% fall in the corresponding period in the previous year.

The sharp rally in Q1 2026 was driven by supply disruptions and elevated geopolitical risk. Rising tensions in key producing regions, including the Middle East and Eastern Europe, heightened fears of export constraints, while bottlenecks at critical transit routes such as the Strait of Hormuz added to supply concerns. At the same time, continued output discipline from the OPEC+ alliance further tightened the market.

On the demand side, resilient activity in major economies like the U.S. and India, alongside stronger travel and seasonal consumption, supported higher crude demand.

As a result, energy equities outperformed sharply, with oil majors and upstream companies benefiting from stronger earnings momentum and increased investor inflows..

Industries performance:

In Q1, the Energy Equipment & Services (SP500-101010) industry advanced 29.2%, while the Oil Gas & Consumable Fuels (SP500-101020) added about 35% in the quarter.

Fund Flows

XLE reported fund inflow of $6.31B during the last quarter. 

Image source: State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF

Q1 top performers:

  • APA (APA) +73.5%
  • Texas Pacific Land (TPL) +65.23%
  • Occidental Petroleum (OXY) +58.07%
  • Valero Energy (VLO) +51.78%
  • Marathon Petroleum (MPC) +50.14%

Expand Energy (EXE) was the only energy stock that slipped this quarter, with a 0.53% decline. 

What Quantitative Measures Say

The Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLE) has a Seeking Alpha Quant Rating of Strong Buy (4.5/5). Momentum, expenses and liquidity stayed strong at A+, while risks stayed at D+.

What Analysts Expect

Seeking Alpha analysts were cautious and gave XLE a Hold rating. Four out of the nine analysts surveyed by Seeking Alpha in the last 90 days considered it a Buy, while five recommended the ETF as a Hold.

data.title: Amazon to apply 3.5% fuel surcharge to third-party deliveries
data.description:
To offset higher operating costs due to increased fuel prices, Amazon (AMZN) is implementing a  3.5% fuel surcharge to fulfillment services for U.S. and Canadian third-party sellers on its platform.

Beginning April 17, Amazon (AMZN) will add the fee to Fulfillment by Amazon ((FBA)) orders in the U.S. and Canada, as well as to Remote Fulfillment FBA from the U.S. into Canada, Mexico, and Brazil.

Starting on May 2, a surcharge will also apply to Buy with Prime in the U.S. and Multi-Channel Fulfillment ((MCF)) in the U.S. and Canada. 

The surcharge will be based on fulfillment fees, not the sale price of items sold. On average, this translates into an additional $0.17 per unit for all U.S. FBA.  

With the cost of fuel increasing by as much as 65% since the start of the war with Iran, FedEx (FDX) and UPS (UPS) have also implemented fuel surcharges, both of which base the additional charge on the weekly published national average for a gallon of diesel fuel.

data.title: Does cooling AI data centers pay off? This auto supplier is up 1300% in five years
data.description:
Modine Manufacturing Company (MOD) has rallied 1345% over the last five years to easily top auto-related stocks and stand as one of the top performers in the overall consumer sector. The return for Modine (MOD) is more than triple other high-performing auto sector stocks such as Garrett Motion (GTX), Allison Transmission (ALSN), O'Reilly Automotive (ORLY), Ferrari (RACE), Tesla (TSLA), and AutoZone (AZO).

Notably, the company executed a big mix shift toward higher-growth, higher-margin thermal management markets, especially data centers, while reducing dependence on weaker vehicle end markets. Last year, Modine (MOD) management said its record results were led by the data center business.

Modine (MOD) also benefited from the AI trade as investors stopped valuing it like a cyclical auto supplier and started valuing it like a growth company tied to data center cooling and thermal management. Compared with peers such as BorgWarner (BWA), Dana (DAN), Aptiv (APTV), and other auto-supplier names, Modine (MOD) has had a much more favorable growth backdrop because a significant mix of its business is now tied to data-center cooling rather than purely to light vehicle or truck production. 

Looking ahead, Modine (MOD) recently announced the launch of a new 3-megawatt turbo chilled chiller platform that is specifically designed to provide advanced free cooling heat rejection for high-density next-generation GPU-powered data centers.

data.title: Eli Lilly launches Zepbound KwikPen on Ro
data.description:
• Telehealth platform Ro announced on Friday that it has partnered with Eli Lilly (LLY) to launch KwikPen, a prefilled injection pen containing multiple doses of the pharma giant’s blockbuster obesity drug Zepbound.
  • The four-dose single-patient use device, approved by the FDA earlier this year, was launched on Lilly’s (LLY) direct-to-consumer site in February, with its lowest dose level starting at $299 per month.
  • In March, Amazon Pharmacy (AMZN) began to offer KwikPen for same-day delivery. Ro said it is launching the multi-dose device for cash-paying customers, with the 2.5 mg starter dose and the 5 mg dose available at $299 and $399 per month, respectively.
  • Patients who complete refills within 45 days will be able to buy higher doses, 7.5 mg, 10 mg, 12.5 mg, and 15 mg, at a manufacturer-discounted rate of $449 per month.

data.title: Maine governor backs millionaire tax as Senate primary tightens
data.description:
Maine Governor Janet Mills has shifted her stance on taxing top earners, now supporting a proposed levy on millionaires as she trails in a competitive Democratic Senate primary.

The governor on Thursday endorsed a 2% surtax on high incomes included in a budget plan advancing through Maine’s legislature. The move marks a reversal from 2024, when she vetoed a similar proposal and warned it could drive wealthy residents out of the state.

Her change in position comes as Mills lags behind challenger Graham Platner, a political newcomer who has built his campaign around raising taxes on the rich. Platner is seeking the Democratic nomination to face Republican Senator Susan Collins in November, a race Democrats view as critical to gaining a Senate majority.

Under the current proposal, the surtax would apply to income above $1 million for individuals and $1.5 million for couples, pushing Maine’s top rate above 9%. Mills now argues the measure is needed to sustain funding for priorities like health care and education while supporting property tax relief.

She said preserving those investments is especially important given economic uncertainty tied to federal policy shifts.

Maine would join states such as Massachusetts and Washington in pursuing higher taxes on top earners, even as it has recently attracted higher-income residents. Federal data shows the state saw a net influx of wealthier households in 2023.

Mills had previously cautioned that relying on a small pool of high-income taxpayers could be risky and temporary. She also argued earlier this year that state-level tax hikes might prompt affluent residents, particularly retirees, to relocate to lower-tax states.

Platner, meanwhile, continues to push for steeper taxes on the ultra-wealthy. He has framed the issue as a test of Democratic priorities, arguing that many voters feel the party has become too aligned with affluent donors instead of working Americans.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Regeneron gains approval of every 20 week dosing for Eylea HD
data.description:
• The US FDA has approved dosing of Eylea HD (aflibercept) as infrequently as every 20 weeks for wet age-related macular degeneration and diabetic macular edema (DME) after one year of successful response with traditional dosing.
  • That schedule is once a month for the first three months, followed by once every two to three months. 
  • The extended dosing schedule means most patients, after a year of treatment, will only need two or three doses annually.
  • Approval of the extended dosing was based on two-year data from the PULSAR trial in wet AMD and the PHOTON trial in DME that showed sustained efficacy.

data.title: We’re not seeing AI productivity gains yet – Apollo Global Management’s Torsten Slok
data.description:
Artificial intelligence has yet to deliver the broad economic productivity gains that many have anticipated, according to Torsten Slok, chief economist at Apollo Global Management.

“At this point, we’re just not seeing that yet,” Slok said in an interview with CNBC, noting that the debate now centers on when and how these gains will finally materialize in the economy.

Slok’s analysis examined the 20-24 age demographic, a group many feared would be among the first impacted by AI-driven job displacement. However, the data shows that youth unemployment trends currently mirror the broader labor market rather than revealing any AI-specific impact.

“It is really not the case that people between 20 and 24 years old are having a harder time finding a job than other demographics,” Slok explained.

Despite the current lack of evidence, Slok remains confident that AI’s economic impact will eventually become apparent.

“I do believe that over time, it will begin to show up in the economy in a broader sense, and we will begin to see more gains to productivity and, with more gains to the economy more broadly,” he said. The question of when and how these productivity improvements will emerge remains a key issue for market participants.

Slok also addressed the recent spike in oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM) following President Trump’s threats of military action against Iran, characterizing the inflationary pressure as a temporary shock to the economy.

He pointed to recent strong economic data from ADP and ISM, along with improving consumer confidence, as evidence of underlying economic strength even as energy prices add short-term pressure to inflation.

The chief economist emphasized that the U.S. is better positioned to weather oil price volatility than other regions because it is a net oil exporter, in contrast to Europe, which remains a net oil importer.

Even if prices temporarily rise to $125 a barrel, Slok estimates the impact would be relatively muted, adding only about 0.1% to core inflation and 0.1% to unemployment.

Looking ahead, Slok expects that as military operations wind down, oil prices will stabilize and inflationary pressures will ease.

He noted that alternative oil sources, including increased U.S. production, could help restore supply and push energy prices lower. Despite current market turbulence, Slok maintains that the U.S. economy remains resilient enough to absorb these short-term pressures.

Tech ETFs: (VGT), (XLK), (IYW), (FTEC), (IXN), and (RSPT).

Artificial Intelligence/Robotics ETFs: (AIQ), (BOTZ), (DTEC), (WTAI), (XAIX), (WISE), (GINN), (ROBT), (TECB), (XT), (THNQ), and (CHAT).

data.title: Atlas Lithium's Neves project tapped for potential funding under U.S.-Japan partnership
data.description:
Atlas Lithium (ATLX) up 5.2% in Thursday's trading after saying its Neves lithium project in Brazil has been included in the recently signed Japan-U.S. Critical Minerals Project Cooperation agreement, as its project was named in the joint fact sheet submitted by the governments for funding consideration.

The joint fact sheet follows the U.S.-Japan Critical Minerals Investment Ministerial held on March 14 in Tokyo among the U.S. Department of the Interior, the U.S. Department of Energy, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, and Japan's Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry, as well as the summit between President Trump and Japan's Prime Minister Takaichi on March 19.

Atlas Lithium (ATLX) noted its inclusion in the joint fact sheet given the company's existing strategic partnership with Mitsui, which made a $30M strategic investment in the company in 2024 and entered into an offtake agreement for the purchase of lithium concentrate from the Neves project.

Now in the pre-production stage, the project involves the processing of ore from the Neves deposit in a dense media separation plant to produce lithium concentrates; once operational, it is expected to produce 300K metric tons/year.

data.title: Lindsay sees MENA project deliveries continuing through Q3 and Q4, while galvanizing facility targets early 2027 startup
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lindsay Corporation (LNN) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “We are closely monitoring the situation with our top priority remaining the safety of our employees and partners in the region.” (President, CEO & Director Randy Wood) Wood said the MENA market is “a strong source of growth” for international irrigation and that deliveries tied to the company’s “most recent project are meaningful to our revenue,” adding, “The project remains on schedule, and our supply chains are currently operating without disruption.”
  • “In North America, our irrigation business customers continued to delay large capital purchases given current farm economics, which, as expected, resulted in lower unit sales volumes in the quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Wood) He said international irrigation was “flat to slightly down year-over-year,” citing “lower sales volumes in Brazil and the timing of project revenue in the MENA region,” and added that in Brazil “high interest rates and limited access to credit continue to constrain growers’ ability to finance capital equipment purchases.”
  • “Total revenues for the second quarter of fiscal 2026 were $157.7 million, a decrease of 16% compared to $187.1 million in the prior year.” (CFO & Senior VP Samuel Hinrichsen) He said operating income was $13 million and net earnings were $12.0 million, or $1.15 per diluted share, and added that the year-over-year decline reflected “lower operating income and a higher effective tax rate.”
  • “During the quarter, we introduced 2 new products” at an industry trade show: “The AlphaGuard channeling device” and “The Road Runner” truck-mounted attenuator. (President, CEO & Director Wood) He framed the launch as reflecting “our investment in innovation and the growing demand for efficient and safe roadway solutions.”

Outlook

  • “We expect softer market conditions to persist in the near term in North America.” (President, CEO & Director Wood) He said the company is “not seeing the traditional pickup in spring order volume” and tied the trough to “greater clarity around trade impacts, profitability and resolution in the Middle East.”
  • “We do not expect any meaningful market recovery until the new crop plan is released in July.” (President, CEO & Director Wood) He said Brazil’s near-term recovery depends on “grower response to the new crop plan and the availability of attractive financing,” and management planned to monitor sentiment at the Agri Show “later this month.”
  • “We will continue delivering the large MENA project throughout the third and fourth quarters.” (President, CEO & Director Wood) He also said the new galvanizing operation in Lindsay, Nebraska is “expected to come online in early 2027.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management’s outlook kept the “trough” framing for North America intact, but added a new variable around the Middle East conflict and emphasized the July timing of Brazil’s crop plan as a gating factor for recovery.

Financial Results

  • Q2 revenue and earnings were $157.7 million and $1.15 per diluted share vs. revenue estimate $171,391,250 and EPS estimate $1.68. (Company results from CFO remarks; estimates from analystsEstimates)
  • “Irrigation segment revenues for the second quarter, were $141.2 million, a decrease of 5% compared to the $148.1 million in the prior year.” (CFO & Senior VP Hinrichsen) He reported North America irrigation revenue of $71 million and international irrigation revenue of $70.2 million.
  • “In our Infrastructure segment, revenues for the second quarter were $16.5 million compared to $38.9 million in the prior year.” (CFO & Senior VP Hinrichsen) He attributed the year-over-year decline to “the absence of the $20 million Road Zipper project,” while adding, “Excluding the Road Zipper project, revenues were up 6%, driven by continued growth in road safety products.”
  • “At the end of the second quarter, our total available liquidity was $236.1 million,” including $186.1 million in cash and cash equivalents, and “$50 million available” under the revolver. (CFO & Senior VP Hinrichsen) He also said the company “complet[ed] $25 million of share repurchases.”

Q&A

  • Nathan Jones, Stifel: asked about softer irrigation margins and whether competition was driving pricing pressure; CFO Hinrichsen said margin compression was mainly from “fixed cost deleverage,” plus “slightly unfavorable” regional mix and “input price inflation,” and CEO Wood added, “We would still say a rational pricing environment in general,” while noting smaller competitors can “ratchet up the pricing intensity” and that Lindsay is “not going to use pricing as a method to drive market share increases.”
  • Nathan Jones, Stifel: asked if the MENA project could slip and whether conflict could delay awards; CEO Wood said the project is proceeding “provided this is not a prolonged conflict,” and added, “If this goes months or several quarters, then we may have a different answer for you.”
  • Ryan Connors, Northcoast: asked how to reconcile higher average selling prices with margin pressure and whether Lindsay is walking away from underpriced business; CEO Wood said, “we certainly have a walkaway plan,” and added that cost “exceeded the pricing opportunities we were able to get in the market.”
  • Ryan Connors, Northcoast: asked whether corn-to-soy acreage shifts affect demand; CEO Wood said, “we're largely indifferent” for machine sales but would watch macro impacts on commodity pricing and profitability.
  • Ryan Connors, Northcoast: asked if infrastructure margin is the run-rate without a large Road Zipper project; CFO Hinrichsen said the “sheer magnitude” of Road Zipper drives mix and absorption, and that without it margin is “closer to what we've seen right now.”
  • Ryan Connors, Northcoast: asked for an update on Nebraska investments and margin impact into FY 2027; CEO Wood said, “our tube mill is up and running,” and the galvanizing facility is “on track” to come online “near the end of the calendar year, sometime in the first portion of our fiscal 2027 year,” while adding that near-term gains may be “eaten up by the incremental depreciation.”
  • Brett Kearney, American Rebirth Opportunity: asked about longer-term food-security project opportunities after the Middle East conflict; CEO Wood said Iran “is not a big grain producer,” and that outcomes depend on “duration,” while maintaining Lindsay is “still active in the region.”
  • Trevor Sahr, William Blair: asked how Brazil outlook evolved and how certain lower financing rates are; CEO Wood said Brazil remains “very, very bullish” long term, but “nothing is guaranteed,” and cited last year’s crop plan at “about 12.5%” with projections “well under that.”
  • Trevor Sahr, William Blair: asked about gross margin drivers and MENA project margin; CFO Hinrichsen pointed to “fixed cost deleverage,” “international mix,” and “input price” risks, and said MENA project margins are “comparable to the previous year project.”
  • Jon Braatz, Kansas City Capital: questioned returns from Project Fortify; CEO Wood said the tube mill tranche is designed to “reduce our reliance on labor,” but “we do need to see some market recovery to get the volume leverage,” and confirmed galvanizing won’t be turned over this fiscal year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to skeptical, pressing on margin sustainability and competitive intensity, with questions like “Are you seeing more intense price competition?” (Nathan Jones, Stifel) and “the deleveraging on the margins did kind of catch us by surprise in infrastructure.” (Ryan Connors, Northcoast)
  • Management tone was slightly positive but risk-aware, repeatedly emphasizing execution and control levers while qualifying external uncertainty; CEO Wood said, “Overall, near-term market conditions remain challenging, but we are confident in our ability to execute,” and on the conflict, “If this goes months or several quarters, then we may have a different answer.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, management added more conditional language tied to geopolitical developments and input-cost risk, while keeping a consistent message on North America softness and Brazil credit constraints.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The Q1 call highlighted an $80 million MENA supply agreement “with approximately $70 million of revenue realization this fiscal year,” while Q2 focused on execution and delivery cadence, with CEO Wood stating the company will deliver the project “throughout the third and fourth quarters.”
  • Q1 infrastructure messaging prepared investors for a difficult Q2 comparison due to a prior-year $20 million Road Zipper project; Q2 results and Q&A centered on the realized impact, with CFO Hinrichsen pointing to mix and absorption effects when that project is absent.
  • Q1 described North America as “bouncing along the trough,” and Q2 reiterated soft demand while noting the lack of a “traditional pickup in spring order volume.”
  • Q2 introduced the Middle East conflict as a near-term operational and demand risk factor; Q1 did not address this.

Risks and Concerns

  • “Any future risk will depend on the duration of the conflict and the potential for broader geographic impact.” (President, CEO & Director Wood) He said Lindsay is monitoring supply chains and logistics for MENA deliveries.
  • “In Brazil, high interest rates and limited access to credit continue to constrain growers' ability to finance capital equipment purchases.” (President, CEO & Director Wood) He tied near-term recovery to the July crop plan and financing availability.
  • “Competitive environment” pressures were discussed as input price inflation and heightened pricing intensity in soft markets, with management emphasizing a “strategic approach” and willingness to walk away from uneconomic business. (CEO Wood; CFO Hinrichsen)
  • Project Fortify returns were described as dependent on market recovery, with CEO Wood stating that at current volumes, savings are likely to be “diluted by the incremental impact of the depreciation.”

Final Takeaway

Management described a quarter shaped by soft North America irrigation demand, Brazil credit constraints, and the absence of a prior-year $20 million Road Zipper project, while emphasizing that the large MENA irrigation project remains on schedule and is expected to be delivered through the second half. Executives repeatedly tied margin pressure to fixed-cost deleverage, mix, and input-cost inflation, and positioned Nebraska investments as strategically important but near-term margin-neutral until volume recovers, with the galvanizing facility expected to come online in early 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Price war among Canadian telecoms fueling a "race to the "bottom"—TD Cowen
data.description:
A price war among Canadian telecom providers is fueling a “race to the bottom,” as these cheaper rates haven't translated into increased volume, leading TD Cowen analyst Vince Valentini to pull his remaining Buy rating on the three major players.

Valentini now views Rogers Communications (RCI), BCE Inc. (BCE), and TELUS Corp. (TU) as a Hold and lowered his average revenue per user ((ARPU)) growth estimates for each by 100 basis points across the next seven quarters.

"Enough damage has already been done to justify a more cautious stance with our ARPU growth forecasts," he says. 

For every dollar decline in wireless service revenue, TD Cowen assumes that 70 cents will flow through to EBITDA.

"We have not assumed any increase in net subscriber growth because, in our view, all carriers showing similar price aggression is simply a race to the bottom with no net winner on volume,” Valentini warns.

With today’s downgrades, Cowen does not have a Buy rating on any of the five major Canadian telecommunication or cable names, a first in TD Cowen’s 30 years of coverage.

“We see market wireless pricing dynamics continuing to weigh on sentiment and financial metrics,” Valentini says, and that the aggressive pricing is not translating into increases in subscriber volume across the Canadian telecom industry, instead causing elevated churn and a negative repricing cycle.

Valentini also warns that there is incremental service revenue risk in the area of $50M per incumbent carrier if the recent Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission ((CRTC)) decision banning activation fees cannot be either overturned or somehow offset.

Of the three, the analyst is more concerned about TELUS, anticipating a 30% cut to its dividend by the end of this year. 

In addition to the downgrade to the three, Valentini also lowered his price target for BCE (BCE) by 10% to $37, by 13% for Rogers (RCI) to $56, and by 9.5% for TELUS (TU) to $19.

The downgrade is pressuring shares, with BCE (BCE) and Rogers Communications (RCI) down more than 7%, while TELUS (TU) is 2.5% lower.
